title: Dental Emergencies in Texas: ER vs Your Dentist
seoTitle: Dental Emergencies: ER vs Dentist (Texas Guide)
seoDescription: When to go to the ER for tooth pain vs your dentist vs wait until tomorrow. A clear triage guide for dental emergencies in Texas.
author: Sonria Dentista clinical team
summary: A practical triage guide for dental emergencies in Texas: which symptoms need the ER, which need a same-day dentist, and which can wait until morning. Includes what to do for a knocked-out tooth.

body.text: When a tooth pain wakes you up at 2 am or your kid takes a baseball to the mouth, the first question is the same: ER or wait for the dentist? The right answer depends on the symptoms, and getting it wrong costs you either money (an unnecessary $1,500 ER bill) or worse (a delayed treatment that lets an infection spread). This guide walks through dental emergencies in Texas: what counts as a true emergency, when the ER is the right call, and when a same-day dentist visit is faster, cheaper, and more effective.

We are Sonria Dentista. Our four offices across North and Northeast Texas hold same-day emergency slots every day, and we wrote this guide based on what we actually see in the chair, not what an insurance brochure says.
When you should go to the ER (not the dentist)
There are a few situations where the hospital emergency room is the correct first stop:
Facial swelling that affects breathing or vision

If your face is so swollen that you cannot open one eye, your tongue is swelling, or you are having trouble breathing, go to the ER right now. A dental abscess that has spread to the eye socket, the floor of the mouth, or the airway is a medical emergency. The ER can give IV antibiotics and consult an oral surgeon. A general dental office is not equipped to handle airway emergencies.
Uncontrolled bleeding

If you have bleeding from the mouth that has not stopped after 20 minutes of firm pressure with gauze, especially after a fall or trauma, the ER can stop the bleeding and check for fractures. Most post-extraction bleeding stops with pressure, so this only applies to bleeding that genuinely will not slow down.
Jaw fracture or significant facial trauma

After a car accident, fall, or assault, if you cannot close your mouth normally, the jaw is clearly out of alignment, or you have lost consciousness, go to the ER. They can do CT imaging and rule out skull fracture. Once stable, an oral surgeon can address the jaw. The dentist comes later, after the ER has cleared the medical emergency.
Fever above 102 with mouth pain

A high fever combined with mouth pain suggests an infection that is spreading systemically. The ER can give IV antibiotics fast. A dentist can prescribe oral antibiotics, but if the fever is high and you feel genuinely sick, the ER is the right call.
When you should call your dentist today (not the ER)

The ER is bad at fixing teeth. They will give you antibiotics and pain medication, then send you home with a referral. They cannot drill, fill, extract, or place a crown. So if your problem is mechanical (broken tooth, lost filling, severe pain from a single tooth), the dentist is faster, cheaper, and actually solves the problem. ER visit cost: $400 to $1,200. Same-day dentist visit cost: $89 to $400.
Severe toothache (sharp, throbbing, will not let you sleep)

Severe pain from a single tooth almost always means an exposed nerve, deep decay, or an abscess. The dentist can do an X-ray, drain the abscess if needed, start antibiotics, and either save the tooth (root canal) or remove it (extraction). The ER cannot do any of those things. They will give you antibiotics and pain meds and tell you to see a dentist tomorrow.
Knocked-out tooth (avulsion)
An adult tooth knocked completely out can sometimes be reimplanted if you act in the first 30 to 60 minutes. The dentist is the right call, not the ER.
What to do, in order:

items: Pick up the tooth by the crown (the white part), never by the root.
Rinse it gently with milk or saline. Do not scrub it. Do not use soap.
If you can, place the tooth back into the socket and bite gently on a clean cloth to hold it.
If reimplanting is not possible, put the tooth in a cup of cold milk or saliva (in your cheek pouch). Do not put it in water.
Call the dentist immediately. Drive there now. Time is the single biggest factor in saving the tooth.

body.text: Baby teeth are different. If a baby tooth is knocked out, do not try to put it back. Call the dentist for an exam, but a baby tooth that comes out early is usually not reimplanted because of risk to the developing adult tooth underneath.
Broken or fractured tooth

A piece of tooth chipped off, or the tooth split. Same-day dentist visit. We can usually do a composite repair, a crown, or a root canal plus crown depending on the depth of the fracture. Bring the broken piece if you can find it, in milk.
Lost crown or filling
Bring the piece if you have it. We can usually re-cement a crown the same day for $80 to $150. A lost filling needs a replacement, usually a 30 to 45 minute visit.
Visible pus or a bump on the gum

A pimple-like bump on the gum, especially if it drains and tastes bad, is an abscess. It will not heal on its own. Same-day dentist visit. We drain it, take an X-ray, and start antibiotics. If left alone, the infection can spread and turn into the kind of swelling that does send you to the ER.
Persistent pain after a recent dental procedure

Pain after an extraction or a root canal is normal for 2 to 3 days, peaking around day 2. Pain that gets worse after day 3 or 4 is not normal and usually means dry socket, infection, or a complication. Call your dentist same-day.
When it can wait until tomorrow
Some things feel scary in the moment but are safe to handle the next business day:
Dull toothache that responds to ibuprofen
If ibuprofen takes the edge off and you can sleep, you can wait until morning. Take ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 to 8 hours, drink plenty of water, and avoid extreme hot or cold drinks.
Small chip with no pain
A tiny chip on a front tooth with no pain or sharp edge can wait. Avoid biting on that tooth and call the next day.
Loose tooth in an adult, no trauma
A slightly mobile adult tooth without recent trauma usually means gum disease and needs evaluation, but is not an emergency. Schedule within a week.
Mild gum bleeding when brushing
Common with gingivitis. Schedule a cleaning. Not an emergency.

body.title: First-aid kit for dental emergencies
body.text: Keep these at home: ibuprofen, acetaminophen, gauze, dental wax (for sharp edges), a small container with a lid, and your dentist's after-hours number. For most dental emergencies, this kit plus a same-day visit handles the situation.
How fast can you actually get a same-day dentist appointment?

Most general dental offices that say they do emergencies hold a few slots open each day. The earlier you call, the better your chance of getting one. At Sonria, if you call before noon, we can almost always see you the same day at one of our four offices. After 2 pm the schedule is usually full and we book you for first thing the next morning.

If your regular dentist is closed (Sunday, after hours, holidays), look for an emergency dental clinic in your area. They usually charge $150 to $300 for the after-hours visit on top of the procedure cost.
What to do tonight if you cannot get to a dentist until morning
Most dental pain at 11 pm has to wait until morning. Here is how to make the night bearable:

items: Ibuprofen 600 mg with food, every 6 to 8 hours. Add acetaminophen 500 mg in between for stronger pain relief (do not exceed daily maximums).
Cold compress on the cheek for 15 minutes on, 15 off, to reduce swelling.
Sleep with your head elevated on an extra pillow to reduce throbbing.
Do not put aspirin directly on the gum. It burns the tissue and does not help.
Do not apply heat. Heat increases blood flow and worsens swelling.
Salt water rinses (one teaspoon in a cup of warm water) several times can ease soreness.

body.text: How much do dental emergencies cost?
Cash prices in Texas in 2026:

items: Limited emergency exam plus targeted X-ray: $89 to $149.
Composite repair on a chipped tooth: $180 to $350.
Drainage of abscess plus antibiotics: $150 to $300, plus the cost of root canal or extraction to follow.

body.text: ER cost for a comparable visit: $400 to $1,200, and you usually still need a dentist visit afterward.
Frequently asked questions
Will antibiotics fix my tooth pain?

Antibiotics treat infection, not pain. They can reduce swelling and make a tooth less painful temporarily, but the underlying problem (decay, abscess, fracture) is still there. Antibiotics buy time, they do not fix teeth. You still need the dentist.
How long can I wait after a knocked-out tooth?

Best chance of saving the tooth: reimplanted within 30 minutes. Reasonable chance: within 60 minutes. After 2 hours, the success rate drops sharply. Cold milk and saliva are the best storage. Drive directly to the dentist or ER if no dentist is available.
Should I take my kid to the dentist or pediatrician for a baby tooth issue?

Most baby tooth issues (chip, fall, mild pain) go to the dentist. The pediatrician can help with general health, fever, or facial swelling. If the kid is acting sick on top of the dental issue, see both.
What if I do not have insurance?

Most dental offices, including ours, treat uninsured patients with transparent cash pricing and financing options. Our $79.99 new patient special covers a full exam plus X-rays. From there, you only pay for the specific treatment you need.
Need same-day care today?

Sonria holds emergency slots every day across all four offices: Arlington, Grand Prairie, Paris, and Commerce. Bilingual team, transparent pricing, and almost always same-day availability if you call before noon. Call (682) 284-1120.
